text.title: django-selectors Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for selector functions in HackSoft-style Django projects. Each is enforceable by `scripts/validate-django-selectors.py` or by a pytest-django test.

rule.statement: Every selector function MUST take keyword-only arguments (signature starts with `*,`). List selectors MUST be annotated to return `QuerySet[Model]`. Single-object selectors MUST be annotated to return `Model` and MUST raise `Model.DoesNotExist` (or a domain-specific 404) when nothing matches — they MUST NOT return `None`.
rule.rationale: Keyword-only signatures make call sites self-documenting and prevent positional misuse when arguments grow. QuerySet return enables further chaining without re-issuing a query. Raising DoesNotExist matches Django's own contract; returning None forces every caller to do None-checks that get forgotten.

rule.statement: The selector MUST include every `select_related(...)` for FK/OneToOne and every `prefetch_related(...)` for M2M / reverse FK / GFK that any caller will dereference. Use `Prefetch(queryset=..., to_attr=...)` when the related queryset needs its own filter, ordering, or inner select_related. Callers (views, serializers, tasks) MUST NOT add their own `select_related` / `prefetch_related` on top.
rule.rationale: Centralising prefetch ownership guarantees the optimisation cannot drift between callers. If serializers add their own prefetches, the next caller forgets and reintroduces N+1.

rule.statement: Existence checks MUST use `qs.exists()`. Forbid `qs.count() > 0`, `len(qs) > 0`, and `bool(list(qs))` for that purpose. Use `qs.annotate(...)` for in-query aggregations; use `F("field")` for atomic field references in filters and updates.
rule.rationale: `.exists()` translates to `SELECT 1 ... LIMIT 1` — O(1) cost. `.count()` issues `COUNT(*)` — O(N) on large tables. `len(qs)` materialises every row into Python memory. F() expressions evaluate server-side, eliminating the read-modify-write race.

rule.statement: Selectors MUST be read-only. No `.save()`, no `.create()`, no `.update()`, no `.delete()`, no transaction state changes, no Celery `.delay()`. Write operations live in `services.py`. Side-effectful caching (cache.set) is allowed inside the selector when the cache layer is read-through; otherwise belongs in a service.
rule.rationale: The read/write split is the entire point of selectors vs services. A selector that writes makes test isolation impossible (every call to a selector mutates DB) and breaks the mental model.

rule.statement: Every list selector MUST have a pytest using `django_assert_num_queries(N)` that locks the expected query count under representative fixture data. Single-object selectors with related data MUST do the same for detail-shaped tests.
rule.rationale: Without the lock, accidental N+1 reintroduction (e.g. removing a prefetch during refactor) is invisible until production. Locking N inside the selector test, not the view test, isolates the failure to the right module.
